name: Refuse the Split: Why 'Meet in the Middle' Is Often the Worst Outcome
description: 'Let's just split the difference' sounds fair and ends the call quickly — and that is precisely why it usually leaves money on the table. This skill teaches when to refuse the split, when to redirect to non-price concessions, and when accepting the split is actually correct.
disclaimer: Refusing the split has social cost — it signals you're not 'reasonable' in a folk sense. The cost is real but usually small relative to the dollars. Only refuse when the dollars justify the friction.

context.when_to_use: Mid-to-late in a price negotiation when the counterparty proposes meeting halfway between two anchors. Especially relevant when the midpoint represents real dollars and the relationship is transactional or recurring.
preconditions: Two anchors are on the table (yours and theirs).
You have at least one non-price dimension you could trade on (term, scope, payment timing, references, exclusivity).
You're not in a hurry to close — refusing the split adds 5-15 minutes to the call.

principles: 'Meet in the middle' rewards the more aggressive anchor. If they anchored extreme and you anchored reasonable, splitting the difference gives them 70% of the spread they wanted.
The split is a heuristic for fairness, not a measurement of value. Both sides agreeing 'this is fair' does not mean it's optimal — it means the call ends fast.

Splitting is highest-cost when it terminates exploration of OTHER dimensions. Once you've split on price, you've also closed the door on negotiating term, support, payment, and scope — those would have moved if you'd refused the split.
Refusing the split is NOT being unreasonable. It's saying 'the price is one of many things; let's solve more of them'.
Sometimes the split IS the right move: tiny gap, relationship matters, time-cost of further negotiation exceeds the gap. Recognize these and accept cleanly.
Never propose the split yourself. Make THEM propose it; that gives you the option to refuse, redirect, or accept.

phrases.polite_refuse: Thanks for the offer to meet in the middle — but I'm not quite there yet on the dollars. What if we held the price closer to my number and you covered [shipping / setup / training / month-one] instead?
phrases.redirect_to_term: Splitting on price is one option — but what if I committed to [longer term / multi-year / autopay] in exchange for getting closer to my number?
phrases.redirect_to_scope: Rather than splitting on price, what if we trimmed [feature/scope item] and held the price where I need it?
phrases.redirect_to_timing: What if I paid net-15 / annual upfront / in 30 days, and we held the price?
phrases.hold_my_number: I get it, and I want to make this work — but my number is my number for [reason]. What else might we put on the table to bridge it?
phrases.accept_the_split_cleanly: You know what, given how close we are — let's do it. Read me back the final terms.
phrases.name_the_pattern: I notice we keep landing back at the midpoint — but the midpoint is where both of us walk away slightly unhappy. Can we try one more pass with something other than price moving?

tactics.name: Refuse politely, redirect to a cheap-to-them dimension
tactics.when: Rep proposes meeting in the middle on price.
tactics.script: Use `polite_refuse` or one of the redirect phrases. Pick the dimension cheapest to the rep — many reps can give 'free shipping' or 'waived setup fee' more easily than a price cut, because those don't hit their pricing-discount KPI.

tactics.name: Hold your number until they've traded across dimensions twice
tactics.when: Rep offers a small price move plus a verbal 'meet me in the middle'.
tactics.script: Use `hold_my_number`. The rule: never split on price until you've traded across non-price dimensions at least twice. If you've done so and they're still stuck, then split — but knowing you've extracted the cross-dimension value first.

tactics.name: Name the pattern when the rep keeps proposing splits
tactics.when: Third or fourth time the rep proposes splitting.
tactics.script: Use `name_the_pattern`. Naming the pattern interrupts it. The rep will either reveal real authority limits ('I literally can't do anything but splits') or pivot to creative concessions.

tactics.name: Accept the split when it's actually right
tactics.when: Gap is small (under 5% of total deal), relationship is long-term, time-cost of further negotiation exceeds the gap.
tactics.script: Use `accept_the_split_cleanly`. Take the split warmly — banked goodwill pays out on next renewal. Don't grudgingly accept; that wastes the relationship dividend.

tactics.name: Trade SMALL price moves for LARGE non-price gains
tactics.when: Rep offers half-the-difference; you want to give some but not split.
tactics.script: Counter: 'I can move a quarter of the way on price IF you can move on [term / scope / payment]. Otherwise I stay at my number.' Asymmetric trade — small price concession for large structural win.

decision_tree.description: When 'meet in the middle' is proposed, run this tree BEFORE accepting. The rep is signaling end-of-call energy; you have one window to redirect.

branches.if_you_hear: 'Let's just meet in the middle.' / 'Let's split the difference.'
branches.interpretation: Standard offer. Rep wants to close. Now decide: is the gap big enough to fight for?
branches.say: Compute: gap × your discount factor. If >5% of total deal AND you have non-price dimensions to trade, use `polite_refuse` + redirect. If <5% AND relationship matters, `accept_the_split_cleanly`.
branches.do_not: Do not auto-accept. Do not auto-refuse. The 5% heuristic decides.

branches.if_you_hear: 'I can come up X if you come down Y' (asymmetric move).
branches.interpretation: Better than a midpoint split. They've moved more than you. Decide whether to mirror their move or trade non-price.
branches.say: Mirror the larger number ('Y?') — buys time, may pull them further. Then propose `redirect_to_term` to capture remaining gap on non-price.
branches.do_not: Do not reflex-counter their proportional move. Treat their move as new information, not a binding template.

branches.if_you_hear: 'This is the best I can do — let's just meet in the middle.'
branches.interpretation: Closing pressure + appeal to fairness. Likely 1-2 more moves available.
branches.say: Use `hold_my_number`: 'I hear you, but my number is my number for [reason]. What else could move?'
branches.do_not: Do not buy 'best I can do' on first delivery. It usually isn't.

branches.if_you_hear: 'I have to get a sign-off if I move further' / 'I'm at the limit'.
branches.interpretation: Real authority constraint, possibly. Escalation is the right call now.
branches.say: Trade the split for an escalation: 'I get that — what if you bring in your manager and we explore non-price options together?'
branches.do_not: Do not accept the split as a way to spare them the escalation. The escalation produces more value than the split.

branches.if_you_hear: Tone shifts to 'frustrated but trying' after your refusal of the split.
branches.interpretation: Rep is invested in closing. Good. They will work harder.
branches.say: Label it: 'It sounds like you really want to make this work — I do too. Let's solve it together.' Then redirect to a non-price dimension.
branches.do_not: Do not feel guilty and accept. Their frustration is leverage, not a moral signal.

branches.if_you_hear: Gap is small (<3% of total) AND rep proposes split.
branches.interpretation: Take it. Time is more valuable than the remaining gap.
branches.do_not: Do not refuse on principle. Refusing a small split costs the relationship more than it earns.

branches.if_you_hear: Rep proposes split number 3 in a row, same offer each time.
branches.interpretation: Rep has hit their authority ceiling and is repeating themselves.
branches.say: Pivot entirely. `name_the_pattern` + redirect to non-price OR escalate to supervisor.
branches.do_not: Do not keep refusing identically — same input from you produces same output from them.

boundaries: Do NOT refuse a split when the gap is trivial and the relationship is long-term. Bad math.
Do NOT lecture the rep on why splitting is suboptimal — just redirect.
Do NOT use 'meet in the middle' phrasing yourself. Force them to propose it; you keep the option to refuse.
Do NOT refuse split #1 and then accept split #2 at the same number — you've trained them you fold under repetition.
success_signals: Rep proposes a non-price concession unprompted ('what about waiving the setup fee?').
Rep offers an asymmetric move ('I can come down X, can you come up Y?').
Rep loops in a supervisor or escalates to higher authority.
Rep accepts your number with a face-saving condition you can grant cheaply (longer term, autopay).
Final outcome captures both: closer to your price target AND a non-price win you didn't have before.
failure_signals: Rep firmly refuses any non-price discussion ('it's price or nothing').
Call duration > 60 min and you've refused 3 splits with no movement.
You're now 5% further from your target than before refusing — you've burned credibility; consider taking the split before it disappears.
Rep ends the call entirely after your refusal.

exit_strategy.on_success: Once the rep moves on non-price OR splits asymmetrically toward you, lock the deal. Confirm all terms with `commitment_check`.
exit_strategy.on_failure: If refusing the split has stalled the call hard, reset: 'OK, I've been pushing — let me hear what you'd consider truly final, and I'll make a call.' Sometimes the right move is taking the split late and gracefully.

required_user_info: What non-price dimensions the user is empowered to trade (term length, payment terms, scope, exclusivity, references).
User's tolerance for additional call time — refusing the split costs time.
